chunks.heading: What is my Normal Retirement Age (NRA)?
chunks.text: Your Normal Retirement Age (NRA) is the age at which you (as a Fund participant with at least 5 years of contributory service at the date of separation) would be entitled to a retirement benefit under Article 28 of the Fund's Regulations and Rules. Your NRA is defined by the date you last (re-)entered UNJSPF participation, which may or may not coincide with your actual date of employment. Your NRA may be different from your Mandatory Age of Separation. Summary: if you joined the UNJSPF before 1 January 1990, your NRA is 60 years; if you joined between 1 January 1990 and 31 December 2013, your NRA is 62 years; if you joined on or after 1 January 2014, your NRA is 65 years.

chunks.heading: What is my Mandatory Age of Separation (MAS) and how does it relate to my NRA?
chunks.text: Your Mandatory Age of Separation (MAS) is the age at which you, as a staff member, must separate from the service of your organization, regulated by the Staff Regulations and Rules of your employing organization. The UNJSPF has no say in, nor any objection to, your staying in active service after having reached either your MAS or NRA. In some UNJSPF Member Organizations the MAS for active staff is currently 65, in others still 62 — for example, the United Nations introduced a change to its MAS effective January 2018, allowing staff active at that date to work until MAS 65. However, for staff whose NRA was 62 when this MAS change took place, their NRA is NOT affected by the change in MAS; if you continue working beyond NRA 62, you keep contributing to the UNJSPF, increasing future entitlements, but you would not be eligible to receive the pension while still working. If your MAS differs from your NRA, contact the Fund for advice on your benefit options prior to separating.

chunks.heading: What is my Early Retirement Age?
chunks.text: Just like your NRA, your Early Retirement Age for pension purposes is based on the date you last (re-)entered UNJSPF participation and the applicable Regulations and Rules at that time. If you joined the UNJSPF before 1 January 2014, your early retirement age is 55 years; if you joined on or after 1 January 2014, your early retirement age is 58 years.

chunks.heading: What is the Withdrawal Settlement (Article 31)?
chunks.text: If you have LESS than five years of contributory service, this is the only benefit option available. If you have more than five years, you can opt for a withdrawal settlement provided you have not reached your normal retirement age. It is a one-time payment consisting of the reimbursement of your contributions plus 3.25% compound interest. Your own contribution is increased by 10% for each year after your first five years with the Fund, up to a maximum of 100% (ten years of 10% per year increase, obtained after fifteen years of service). If you elect a withdrawal settlement, no other benefit will be paid to you or your survivors. Should you receive a withdrawal settlement and re-join the Fund in the future, you are entitled to restore the period for which the settlement was paid, in accordance with Article 24, but must do so within one year of re-joining the Fund.

chunks.heading: What is the Deferred Retirement Benefit (Article 30)?
chunks.text: If you have five or more years of contributory service and separate before reaching your normal retirement age, you are eligible to elect a deferred retirement benefit — a periodic monthly benefit payable for life, becoming payable at your normal retirement age or, at your request, anytime between your early and normal retirement age (with reduction factors applied if commenced early). For participants who separated before 31 December 1989, no cost-of-living adjustment applies before age 50; for those separating on or after 31 December 1989, no cost-of-living adjustment applies before age 55. A deferred retirement benefit carries potential surviving spouse's benefit(s) but does not carry eligibility to a child benefit.

chunks.heading: What is the Early Retirement Benefit (Article 29)?
chunks.text: If you have at least five years of contributory service and separate after reaching your early retirement age but before your normal retirement age, you can opt for an early retirement benefit, subject to a reduction factor depending on your age at separation and length of contributory service. Up to one-third of the benefit can be commuted into a lump sum, with the balance payable as a reduced monthly pension for life. The early retirement benefit carries both potential survivor's benefits and child benefit, though payment of a child benefit commences only when you reach your Normal Retirement Age, and only for children under age 21 at that time.

chunks.heading: What is the Normal Retirement Benefit (Article 28)?
chunks.text: If you have more than five years of contributory service and separate on or after reaching your normal retirement age, you receive a retirement benefit, with the option to commute up to one-third into a lump sum (reducing the monthly amount). The benefit carries both potential survivor's benefits and child's benefit.

chunks.heading: What happens if I delay my election of benefit or payout (Article 32)?
chunks.text: If you have less than five years of contributory service, you can delay payment of a withdrawal settlement for up to 36 months. If you have five or more years, you can delay your choice of benefit (withdrawal settlement or deferred retirement benefit) for up to 36 months. Should you re-join the Fund within this 36-month deferment period (and no benefit has been paid), your participation is deemed continuous, though the interim period is a break in service that cannot be made pensionable. If you do not re-join the Fund, you must submit your benefit election and payment instructions by the end of the 36 months. If you have less than five years of contributory service and do not submit Payment Instructions within 36 months, it may lead to forfeiture of your benefit if, after a further 24 months, no claim has been made. If you have five or more years and do not submit your election within the 36-month deferment period, the Fund will automatically deem you to have elected a deferred retirement benefit.

chunks.heading: What benefit options are available to me depending on my years of contributory service and age at separation?
chunks.text: Separating with LESS THAN FIVE YEARS of contributory service: Article 31 (Withdrawal settlement) or Article 32 (Deferment of choice or benefit for 36 months). Separating with FIVE OR MORE YEARS of contributory service PRIOR TO REACHING EARLY RETIREMENT AGE: Article 30 (Deferred retirement benefit), Article 31 (Withdrawal settlement), or Article 32 (Deferment for 36 months). Separating with FIVE OR MORE YEARS AFTER REACHING EARLY RETIREMENT AGE but PRIOR TO NORMAL RETIREMENT AGE: Article 29 (Early retirement benefit), Article 30 (Deferred retirement benefit), Article 31 (Withdrawal settlement), or Article 32 (Deferment for 36 months). Separating with FIVE OR MORE YEARS AT NORMAL RETIREMENT AGE: Article 28 (Normal retirement benefit).

chunks.heading: What steps and documents are required to process my separation benefit?
chunks.text: Begin the separation process at least two months before your separation date, and run an estimate using the interactive Estimate tool through Member Self-Service. From your member organization, the Fund must receive: the Separation Notification form (PF4), completed and signed by an authorized payroll/finance officer, detailing pensionable remuneration and contributions since the end of the previous year; and the Separation Personnel Action form (SEPPA), giving your official date of separation. Discrepancies will delay payments — inquire with your member organization first before contacting the Fund. From you: the original applicable Payment Instructions (PI) form with complete bank account information, currency of payment, contact details, and original ink signature (UN Family participants can submit online via MSS). If electing a periodic benefit, the Fund also needs supporting documents as applicable: a copy of your Birth Certificate or valid passport; a copy of your valid photo ID (the UN Laissez-passer is NOT valid); a copy of your marriage certificate(s); a copy of your divorce decree(s); a copy of the birth certificate or valid passport of your current/former spouse(s); a copy of the spouse's official photo ID; a copy of the birth certificate(s) of dependent children under 21; a copy of death certificate(s) for spouse(s)/ex-spouse(s)/child(ren) if applicable; and Form A/2 (Designation of Beneficiary). These documents may have been given to your organization during your career but not necessarily shared with the Fund.

chunks.heading: When can I retire, i.e., start receiving a monthly benefit payment?
chunks.text: You may retire and receive a monthly benefit at any time after reaching early retirement age, provided you have five years or more of contributory service. If you have LESS than five years, you can only take a withdrawal settlement, regardless of age at separation.

chunks.heading: I am about to separate from service. How do I find out the amount of my pension benefit?
chunks.text: You can run estimates using the Benefit Estimate tool through the Fund's Member Self-Service Portal.

chunks.heading: What do I do if I find that my pension payment has not been processed as soon as I expected?
chunks.text: Contact your employing organization to ensure all required documents have been processed. Speak to your local Staff Pension Committee Secretariat (if a Specialized Agency) or the UNJSPF itself (if a UN family organization) to determine the cause of delay.

chunks.heading: Will my benefit be adjusted for inflation?
chunks.text: Your US dollar monthly benefit is adjusted for cost-of-living changes measured by the US Consumer Price Index (currently requiring a minimum 2% movement). For a retiree on the two-track system, the benefit takes into account the CPI of both the US (dollar track) and the country of residence (local track), plus the exchange rate, per the comparison feature of the Pension Adjustment System.

chunks.heading: Can I change my choice of benefit after my initial election has been processed?
chunks.text: No. You cannot change a benefit election once implemented (a payment made) for any reason — the decision is irrevocable once acted upon.

chunks.heading: What happens if I separate from service and wish to transfer my pension rights to the pension plan of my new employer, how may I do so?
chunks.text: Transfer of pension rights is only possible if there is a formal transfer agreement between the UNJSPF and the other pension plan.

chunks.heading: What is the local track or two-track pension system, and would it apply to me?
chunks.text: See the dedicated Two-Track page/booklet for details.

chunks.heading: Will my spouse be entitled to a benefit?
chunks.text: If you were married prior to separation from service, are in receipt of a retirement, early retirement, deferred retirement or disability benefit, and your spouse remained married to you and survives you, they become entitled to a survivor's benefit upon your death. If there are two or more legally recognized spouses, the benefit is divided among them. A former spouse fulfilling the criteria for a divorced surviving spouse's benefit may also be entitled, in which case the benefit is divided between current and former spouses in proportion to the duration of their marriages to you (Articles 34, 35, 35 bis and 35 ter).

chunks.heading: If I marry/remarry after retirement, would my new spouse be entitled to a benefit?
chunks.text: No, a new spouse would not be eligible for a survivor's benefit in the event of your death, unless you purchase a benefit for them through a reduction in your own monthly benefit, by submitting an application to the UNJSPF within 180 days of the date of marriage/remarriage.

chunks.heading: Is my pension different if I am single or married?
chunks.text: No. The amount of your benefit would not be different whether you are married or single.

chunks.heading: Will my children be entitled to benefits?
chunks.text: A child's benefit is payable for unmarried children under 21 (or beyond, if recognized as disabled). If you're in receipt of a retirement benefit, the child's benefit begins at the same time as your own. If in receipt of an early retirement benefit, the child's benefit is payable from when you reach normal retirement age for any child under 21 at that time, unless the child was recognized as disabled, in which case payment can start with your early retirement benefit. A benefit is never payable for a child born after your separation date unless conceived before that date, and is not payable if you took a deferred retirement benefit or withdrawal settlement.

chunks.heading: Is my benefit taxable?
chunks.text: Each country determines whether and to what extent UNJSPF pensions are subject to national taxation; the UNJSPF does not provide tax advice, and makes no tax reimbursements under any circumstances (reimbursement of national income taxes, if any, is done by the former employing organization). The Fund is a qualified plan under US Internal Revenue Code Section 401(a). You can request an annual Statement of Benefits, sent automatically each year after the first request, usually one month after the end of the relevant fiscal year; statements issued after 1 January 2018 are also accessible via the DOCUMENTS tab in Member Self-Service, along with disbursement history under "Disbursements" showing payments, holds, and ASHI deductions.

chunks.heading: What is Member Self-Service and how do I register?
chunks.text: Member Self-Service (MSS) provides up-to-date account information, Annual Statements (since 2004), estimation tools, official UNJSPF forms, benefit payment history, and a document upload tool. To register, you need your Unique Identification Number (UID), your last name as registered with the UNJSPF, your date of birth, and an email account.
